questions.question: Define a distributed system and explain its key characteristic that makes it different from centralized systems.
keyPoints: Distributed system is a collection of independent computers appearing as single coherent system
Key difference: computation and data spread across multiple interconnected machines
Users perceive the system as a single entity despite physical distribution

questions.question: Explain the concept of transparency in distributed systems with examples of different types of transparency.
keyPoints: Transparency hides distribution from users
Types include: access transparency (local/remote access looks same), location transparency (users dont know location), migration transparency (resources can move without user knowledge)
Makes system appear as single centralized computer

questions.question: Differentiate between client-server and peer-to-peer architectures in distributed systems.
keyPoints: Client-Server: central server provides services, clients request services
Peer-to-Peer: all nodes equal, act as both client and server
Client-server has single point of failure risk, P2P is more resilient
Examples: Client-server = web servers, P2P = file sharing networks

questions.question: What is middleware? Explain its role and functions in distributed systems.
keyPoints: Middleware is software layer between applications and operating system
Provides common services for distributed communication
Handles heterogeneity, security, transaction management
Examples: CORBA, DCOM, message queues

questions.question: List and explain three major challenges faced by distributed systems.
keyPoints: Security: network exposure increases vulnerabilities
Synchronization: maintaining consistent data across nodes is difficult
Partial failures: some nodes can fail without affecting entire system
Network issues: latency, partitions affect performance and consistency

questions.question: Explain the CAP theorem and its significance in distributed system design.
keyPoints: CAP: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
Only two of three can be guaranteed simultaneously
During network partitions, must choose between consistency and availability
Important for designing databases and applications

questions.question: What is fault tolerance? How is it achieved in distributed systems?
keyPoints: Fault tolerance: system continues functioning despite component failures
Achieved through redundancy (replication of data and services)
Failover mechanisms switch to backup when primary fails
Checkpoint and recovery techniques save system state

questions.question: Explain the advantages and disadvantages of distributed systems over centralized systems. Provide examples to illustrate your answer.
keyPoints: Advantages: resource sharing, improved reliability (no single point of failure), better scalability, enhanced performance through parallel processing, flexibility and modularity
Disadvantages: more complex to design and manage, security vulnerabilities, network dependency, data consistency challenges, difficult troubleshooting and debugging
Examples: Cloud services (AWS) show advantages, banking systems show complexity challenges

questions.question: Describe the different types of distributed system architectures. Explain the client-server model in detail with its variants.
keyPoints: Types: client-server, peer-to-peer, three-tier, n-tier, hybrid
Client-server variants: two-tier (client directly talks to server), three-tier (presentation, application, data layers)
Three-tier improves scalability and maintainance
Peer-to-peer enables direct resource sharing without central coordination
Each architecture suited for different application requirements

questions.question: Explain what is meant by scalability in distributed systems. Discuss horizontal and vertical scalability with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Scalability: system handles increased load by adding resources
Vertical scaling: add more power to existing machine (CPU, RAM, storage)
Horizontal scaling: add more machines to system
Horizontal more common in distributed systems due to cost-effectiveness and fault tolerance
Examples: Vertical = upgrading server hardware; Horizontal = adding more servers to web farm

questions.question: Discuss the concept of consistency in distributed systems. What are the different consistency models and why is maintaining consistency challenging?
keyPoints: Consistency: all nodes see same data at same time
Models: strict consistency (all reads see most recent write), eventual consistency (updates propagate eventually)
Challenges: network latency, partition tolerance, concurrent updates
Trade-off between consistency and performance
CAP theorem explains why perfect consistency not always possible
